questions.q: In a PID control system, what does the 'I' (integral) term do?
options: Amplifies the rate of change of error
Accumulates historical error to eliminate steady-state offset
Reduces overshoot by damping the response
Adjusts the system gain proportionally
questions.explanation: The integral term sums accumulated error over time, allowing the controller to correct persistent steady-state errors that the proportional term alone cannot fix. If you consistently undershoot a target, the integral term builds up to push harder. The proportional term amplifies current error; derivative term provides damping.

questions.q: What is steady-state error in a closed-loop control system?
options: The error that occurs at system startup
The remaining error between setpoint and actual output when the system reaches equilibrium
Any oscillation in the system output
The maximum allowable error tolerance
questions.explanation: Steady-state error is the final difference between desired and actual values after transients settle. A well-tuned PID with appropriate integral gain drives this to zero. Startup transients are transient response; oscillations are overshoot; tolerance is a separate specification.

questions.q: A robot joint controller uses only proportional gain (P-only control). What problem will likely occur when tracking a constant target?
options: The system will oscillate wildly
There will be a persistent steady-state error
The joint will move too slowly initially
The system response will be overdamped
questions.explanation: P-only control produces steady-state error for constant setpoints unless the setpoint is reached exactly. This error is proportional to the gain but never disappears. An integral term is needed to eliminate this offset. Oscillation suggests too much gain; slow response and overdamping are different issues.

questions.q: What is a feedback loop in a robot control system?
options: A mechanical cable connecting motor to joint
The process of measuring output and adjusting input to reduce error
A software loop that runs indefinitely
A bearing that allows rotation
questions.explanation: A feedback loop continuously measures system output (via sensors), compares it to the desired setpoint, and adjusts the input (motor command) based on the error. This closed-loop structure enables regulation and stability. Cables, bearings, and software loops are unrelated to the control concept.

questions.q: Why is a damping term (derivative, 'D') useful in robot control?
options: It increases the system response speed
It predicts future errors and provides anticipatory correction
It reduces overshoot and improves stability by damping oscillations
It measures motor current
questions.explanation: The derivative term responds to the rate of change of error (dE/dt), providing damping that counteracts overshoot and oscillations. Higher derivative gains produce smoother, less oscillatory responses. It improves stability without increasing speed; prediction requires model-based approaches; current measurement is a separate function.

questions.q: An open-loop control system differs from a closed-loop system in that it:
options: Uses more robust algorithms
Does not use feedback to adjust the control action
Is always more accurate
Requires fewer sensors
questions.explanation: Open-loop systems issue commands without measuring results (e.g., step motor timing). Closed-loop systems measure output and adjust input based on error. Open-loop is simpler but cannot correct for disturbances or model errors. Robustness, accuracy, and sensor requirements depend on design, not loop type.

questions.q: What causes instability (diverging oscillations) in a PID-controlled robot joint?
options: Too much integral gain causing overshoot and wind-up
Friction in the joint bearings
Slow sensor sampling rate
Environmental vibrations
questions.explanation: Excessive integral gain accumulates large corrections, overshooting and reversing direction repeatedly—a classic feedback loop instability. This 'integral wind-up' can cause divergent oscillations. Friction, sensor lag, and vibrations are disturbances but not inherent causes of PID instability if gains are properly tuned.

questions.q: A robot arm joint uses a servo motor with closed-loop control. If the joint lags behind its commanded position, what adjustment might improve performance?
options: Decrease the proportional gain
Increase the proportional gain to respond faster to error
Remove the integral term
Switch to open-loop control
questions.explanation: Lag indicates the proportional gain is too low; the controller isn't responding aggressively enough to position error. Increasing P makes the motor work harder to track commands faster. Decreasing P worsens lag; removing integral loses steady-state correction; open-loop cannot regulate tracking.

questions.q: What is the role of an actuator in a robot control system?
options: To measure position and velocity
To compute control commands
To apply forces or torques that change the robot's state based on control signals
To store sensor data
questions.explanation: Actuators (motors, hydraulic cylinders) are the output devices that convert control signals into mechanical motion. Sensors measure; controllers compute; actuators execute. Data storage is unrelated to real-time control.

questions.q: In a multi-joint robot arm, why is decentralized control (independent PID per joint) sometimes insufficient?
options: Each joint's motion can interfere with others, causing cross-coupling and reduced accuracy
Decentralized control is always sufficient
The algorithm is too computationally expensive
Feedback loops are not possible with multiple joints
questions.explanation: When joints move, they create dynamic coupling: one joint's acceleration affects torques needed by others. Decentralized PID ignores these interactions, leading to tracking errors. Advanced methods like computed-torque or feedback linearization account for coupling. Computational cost and loop feasibility are independent issues.
